title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Clara Evans, who owns a second-hand clothes shop.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're talking to Clara Evans, who owns a second-hand clothes shop. Clara, your shop sells second-hand clothes. What made you open it? || Guest: I have always enjoyed clothes, but I did not want to sell new things that people might only wear once. When I was at university, I bought most of my clothes second-hand because I had little money. Later, [20]I opened the shop because I wanted good clothes to be used again instead of thrown away[/20]. It felt like a business idea that could also reduce waste. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you were starting out? || Guest: The shop looked nice on opening day, but I had not thought enough about sizes. We had lots of small jackets and almost no larger trousers. [21]Customers quickly taught me that a second-hand shop needs a wide range of sizes, not just fashionable pieces[/21]. Now I check every delivery carefully and make sure the rails are useful for different people, ages and body shapes. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about it now? || Guest: Many customers enjoy finding unusual items, but some feel unsure when they enter. They think second-hand clothes may look old. [22]I arrange the shop like a normal clothes store, with outfits shown together[/22]. A clean window display and good lighting help people imagine wearing the clothes today. It makes the shop feel welcoming, not like a box of forgotten things. || Interviewer: Have you changed the way you work since then? || Guest: Online selling has helped, but it also takes time. I photograph special coats or dresses, write short descriptions and answer questions. [23]The most difficult part online is showing the real colour of the clothes in photographs[/23]. A blue jacket can look grey if the light is wrong. I now take photos near the front window in the morning because the colours look more natural. || Interviewer: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work? || Guest: The best moments are personal ones. A student once came in looking for something smart for her first job interview. She did not have much money and felt nervous. [24]We found a jacket that fitted her perfectly, and she came back later to say she had got the job[/24]. I still remember that because the right clothes gave her confidence. || Interviewer: And what are your plans for the future? || Guest: Next, I want to teach people how to look after clothes better. [25]I am planning evening workshops on simple repairs, like sewing on buttons and fixing hems[/25]. Many clothes are thrown away because of tiny problems. If people learn a few basic skills, they can keep favourite items for years, whether they bought them from my shop or somewhere else. || Interviewer: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar? || Guest: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like second-hand clothes shop, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal. || Interviewer: Thank you, that was really interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're talking to Clara Evans, who owns a second-hand clothes shop. Clara, your shop sells second-hand clothes. What made you open it?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I have always enjoyed clothes, but I did not want to sell new things that people might only wear once. When I was at university, I bought most of my clothes second-hand because I had little money. Later, I opened the shop because I wanted good clothes to be used again instead of thrown away. It felt like a business idea that could also reduce waste.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you were starting out?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The shop looked nice on opening day, but I had not thought enough about sizes. We had lots of small jackets and almost no larger trousers. Customers quickly taught me that a second-hand shop needs a wide range of sizes, not just fashionable pieces. Now I check every delivery carefully and make sure the rails are useful for different people, ages and body shapes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about it now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Many customers enjoy finding unusual items, but some feel unsure when they enter. They think second-hand clothes may look old. I arrange the shop like a normal clothes store, with outfits shown together. A clean window display and good lighting help people imagine wearing the clothes today. It makes the shop feel welcoming, not like a box of forgotten things.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you changed the way you work since then?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Online selling has helped, but it also takes time. I photograph special coats or dresses, write short descriptions and answer questions. The most difficult part online is showing the real colour of the clothes in photographs. A blue jacket can look grey if the light is wrong. I now take photos near the front window in the morning because the colours look more natural.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The best moments are personal ones. A student once came in looking for something smart for her first job interview. She did not have much money and felt nervous. We found a jacket that fitted her perfectly, and she came back later to say she had got the job. I still remember that because the right clothes gave her confidence.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: And what are your plans for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Next, I want to teach people how to look after clothes better. I am planning evening workshops on simple repairs, like sewing on buttons and fixing hems. Many clothes are thrown away because of tiny problems. If people learn a few basic skills, they can keep favourite items for years, whether they bought them from my shop or somewhere else.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like second-hand clothes shop, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Thank you, that was really interesting.

questions.question: Why did Clara open her shop?

options.A: She wanted to stop useful clothes being wasted.
options.B: She had studied fashion at university.
options.C: She wanted to sell only designer clothes.

questions.explanation: Clara says she wanted good clothes to be used again instead of thrown away.

questions.question: What did Clara learn after opening?

options.B: She needed clothes in many different sizes.

questions.explanation: Clara learnt that a second-hand shop needs a wide range of sizes.

questions.question: How does Clara make customers feel more confident?

options.A: She gives discounts on old clothes.
options.B: She keeps all clothes in large boxes.
options.C: She displays clothes like a normal shop.

questions.explanation: Clara arranges the shop like a normal clothes store, with outfits shown together.

questions.question: What is hard about selling clothes online?

options.A: describing the size of each item
options.B: showing the colour correctly in photos

questions.explanation: Clara says the most difficult part online is showing the real colour of clothes in photographs.

questions.question: Which customer does Clara remember clearly?

options.A: a student who found clothes for an interview
options.B: a designer who donated several coats
options.C: a tourist who bought a whole outfit

questions.explanation: Clara remembers finding a jacket for a student who later got the job.

questions.question: What does Clara plan to offer?
questions.explanation: Clara is planning evening workshops on simple repairs, like sewing on buttons and fixing hems.
